item.title: The Invisible Tech Running the 2026 World Cup
item.body:
The 2026 FIFA World Cup is the first men's tournament hosted across three countries — the United States, Canada, and Mexico — and the largest in history at 48 teams. But the most consequential upgrades this summer aren't in the stands. They're in the ball, the cameras, and the cloud.

## The connected ball

Inside each match ball sits a motion sensor sampling its position hundreds of times per second. That data streams to officials in real time, capturing the exact instant a player touches the ball — the single hardest thing for a human eye to judge from 50 meters away.

## Semi-automated offside

Offside used to hinge on a linesman's flag and a frozen replay. Now a network of tracking cameras follows every limb of every player, building a skeletal model of the pitch. When an attacker is played through, the system calculates offside in seconds and generates a 3D animation for the big screen. The referee still makes the final call — but the machine hands them the geometry.

## Why three countries makes it harder

Hosting across a continent introduces problems most tournaments never face:

- **Latency at scale.** Video review, ball data, and broadcast feeds must sync across thousands of miles without drift.
- **Travel logistics.** Teams and data crews move between distant host cities; scheduling software juggles rest, climate, and stadium availability.
- **Streaming demand.** Matches now reach more phones than televisions, and peak concurrent viewership can spike into the hundreds of millions in minutes.

## The quiet trade-off

Fans love certainty, but every new system chips away at the sport's human ambiguity — the missed call that fuels a decade of arguments. The technology is faster and more precise than any official could be. The open question, as this tournament unfolds on American soil, is how much of soccer's beautiful chaos we actually want to automate away.
